Makepeace Secrets Exposed!
Q&A Session 2
Module 	7

-- 1 of 31 --

How can I improve my process
for creating information
products?
-- Phillip

-- 2 of 31 --

7 Key Questions to Answer:
1. Who’s your best prospect?
2. What’s the most cost-efficient way to reach
him?
3. What’s his most pressing problem or desire
now?
4. What can you deliver that will solve that
problem or fulfill that desire?
5. What product format has your prospect
shown he’s most willing to accept?
6. Can you achieve your objective in a single
installment or would a series be more
effective?
7. What is he willing to pay?

-- 3 of 31 --

More…
 Have a bump
 Have a forced free trial
 Have a secondary product ready to go

-- 4 of 31 --

In slow economic times,
people are going to be
looking for more ways to
make money.
What are the key
psychological triggers you
hit in down economic times?
-- Matt

-- 5 of 31 --

Four Dominant Emotions
in Tough Times
1. Fear
2. Helplessness
3. Resentment/Anger
4. Desire for security
5. Greed

-- 6 of 31 --

1. Would it be better to position
oneself in B2C over B2B markets right now?
My logic says B2B markets take a harder
beating with a looming downturn of the
economy.
2. I live in Germany and plan to move
to Australia with wife and daughter in
about 2 - 3 years from now.
What would you do: Go into English
speaking markets as a copywriter right now
(while in Germany), or stay in the German
& Swiss market, and then work for German
companies from Australia? It seems the
Australian copywriters write more for the
USA anyway.
3. Are we going towards times where
long sales letters with lots of non-hype
information and valuable background are
going to pull best? 	Especially outside
the newsletter world, for example in the
venture capital/private equity world?
-- Markus

-- 7 of 31 --

1) Can you point me to some good
resources on positioning? (books,
courses, etc.)
2) If you were a newbie copywriter
in today's market...what letter
would you send out to get people
interested? (A sample would be
appreciated.)
3) What do you do when you get
stuck or hit a wall?
4) How many times do you edit your
work?
5) Who are your mentors (now and
in the past)?
-- Steve

-- 8 of 31 --

Damn, 	Clayton. 	You're 	making 	me
feel smart.
Last 	month 	I 	was 	evaluating 	my
business, 	making 	plans 	for 	2008. 	I
knew 	that 	I 	had 	to 	change 	my 	model 	and
become 	something 	of 	a 	"virtual 	VP 	of
Marketing" to small companies.
You've 	confirmed 	this. 	I've 	got 	the
skills 	and 	knowledge 	to 	make 	it 	work.
What 	I 	lack 	is 	a 	clear 	plan 	of 	attack.
How 	do 	I 	find 	these 	companies?
How 	do 	I 	approach 	them?
What 	do 	I 	say... 	send 	them... 	or
offer?
I'm 	willing 	to 	do 	the 	work 	to 	make
it 	happen. 	I'm 	just 	not 	sure 	WHAT 	to
do.
-- Sid

-- 9 of 31 --

What 	would 	be 	your 	first 	step 	in
increasing Average Customer 	Value?
-- Phillip

-- 10 of 31 --

Three Keys to Customer Lifetime Value:
1. Order Frequency
2. Average Order Amount
3. Longevity

-- 11 of 31 --

How 	do 	I 	say, 	in 	essence, 	“Okay,
now 	you’ve 	gotten 	these 	7 	great 	tips 	…
now 	here’s 	what 	you 	need 	to 	buy 	to
keep 	this 	chain 	of 	benefits 	coming
your way?
In 	other 	words, 	how 	do 	I 	go 	from
their friend/benefactor 	to 	selling?
-- Stuart

-- 12 of 31 --

I 	am 	overwhelmed 	with 	so 	many 	marketing,
copywriting newsletters, emails, 	books,
offers, etc.
If 	you 	were 	to 	select 	just 	a 	few
newsletters 	or 	email 	subscriptions 	– 	with
yours 	being 	first 	– 	what 	others 	would 	you
recommend?
-- Larry

-- 13 of 31 --

 Bencivenga Bullets
 The Golden Thread
 Bly.com
 Copywriter’s Roundtable
 CopyBlogger
 Copywriter’s Board
 Warrior Forum

-- 14 of 31 --

What 	new 	ideas 	and 	programs 	are 	you 	working
on 	now, 	or 	planning 	for 	the 	future? 	What
can 	we 	look 	forward 	to 	with 	excitement?
-- Larry

-- 15 of 31 --

Any More 	QUESTIONS?

-- 16 of 31 --

Makepeace Secrets Exposed!
Q&A Session 3
Module 	7

-- 17 of 31 --

•From: Cheryl
Hi Clayton,
Can you give some specific examples ‐ (preferably more
than one) of how we would describe the value we bring to
the partnership to our clients?
If I were putting together a proposal to do the ongoing
copywriting/marketing for a company moving into a new
market, what would that look like with the partnership type
arrangement you've been talking about and how can I
describe it to show the client the true value of what s/he is
getting?

-- 18 of 31 --

• 	From: John
Clayton:
Believability is critical to convince the prospect
to try/buy...
Exactly what steps must be taken to allay the
skeptic mind of the potential buyer so that the
offer is so compelling that they must order it?
God speed

-- 19 of 31 --

•From: Chris
Hi Clayton.
I would like to pitch a Fortune 500 company that's getting their
teeth kicked in by the competition. 	I see a way to stop the bleeding, turn
the tables on their competition, and unleash a radio/print campaign that
would stand a good chance of helping their company do a complete 180.
But the problem is, I don't have a big ad agency behind me. Would
there be a way to get compensated for the ads I wrote, but which would
likely have to be placed by their present ad agency?
I have no idea on how to approach big companies or what kind of
fee arrangement to ask for.

-- 20 of 31 --

•From: Gavriel
Hi Clayton,
I'm in the UK. Although looking at moving to
Spain or Brazil.
I would like to know if and how the partnering
techniques should differ for use in international
markets, such as the UK or Spain.
I don't think the contracts you've provided are
suitable for the UK. At least that's my perspective.

-- 21 of 31 --

•From: Rich
Suppose the copywriter has already written
successful direct mail packages and a catalog for
a client, but is not getting royalties or a
percentage on the backend to the house file.
Since the client is accustomed to "getting a
free ride" so to speak, what would you do now
to convince the client to start paying a profit
percentage?

-- 22 of 31 --

•From: Markus
I have started in partnering. If all works out my
payday will be over 500.000€.
Partner is kind & seems honest, but they are really
slow with their marketing. Their list is far smaller than
I assumed (part of learning curve I guess). Seems
there are some changes in copy necessary.
How long should I keep going with them? Been over 6
weeks in work around the beginning of the year.

-- 23 of 31 --

• 	From: 	Markus
Does 	it 	make 	sense to 	work 	on two 	big
projects 	simultaneously? Seems 	that 	the
good thoughts always 	come 	für project 	A
when you work 	on 	project 	B. 	And vice 	versa.

-- 24 of 31 --

•From: 	Rick
What if 	any changes do 	you 	make 	in your
copy if 	your 	audience is 	a C 	Officer 	of 	a
medium sized 	company? 	My 	son thinks you
need to 	be 	formal 	and business.
I say they all 	put their 	pants on the 	same as
everyone 	else. 	Who 	is 	right?

-- 25 of 31 --

• 	Are 	there 	certain 	industries/niches that 	are
more 	suitable, or 	open‐minded to
partnering?

-- 26 of 31 --

• 	I’m a 	brand new 	copywriter, 	what’s 	the
quickest way 	to 	boost my confidence, 	make
money and get 	a partnering gig going?

-- 27 of 31 --

• 	What are 	some 	of 	the 	downfalls of 	the
partnering model? 	If 	this is 	so great, 	why 	isn’t
everyone 	doing this?

-- 28 of 31 --

• 	What are 	the 	most common 	mistakes 	you’ve
made while 	trying 	to 	structure 	a partnering
deal? 	What are 	deal‐killers?

-- 29 of 31 --

• 	You talk 	about helping Parris 	structure 	his
first 	partnering deal; 	can you 	give 	us more
details about how 	you 	did that?

-- 30 of 31 --

Any 	More 	Questions?

-- 31 of 31 --
